It has been heartening to hear from hundreds of members and readers over the years who tell me how adopting this sort of approach to investing has changed their lives: Allowing them to relax and actually sleep better at night through turbulent economic and financial periods as they focus on their constantly growing cash flow and not so much on how the ephemeral market happens to price the source of that cash flow from week to week or month to month.

Our approach is obviously not for everyone but is merely a viable alternative for those for whom it “works.” I never try to “convert” investors who are happy with the challenges, excitement, and what they see as opportunity in more conventional investing and its emphasis on prices, trading, entry and exit points, and the more technical aspects of managing a strategy focused and largely dependent on capital gains.

Inside the Income Factory members include many who are active “do it yourselfers” in terms of creating and managing their own portfolios. It also includes many who are interested in a somewhat more passive, “set it and forget it” approach, as well as a range of those with a blend of various styles.

To accommodate all of them, I provide two “model” portfolios. One is a “Core” model that developed from my own investing, which has been primarily within an IRA where I could focus mostly on building income through high-yield asset classes without regard to how the income would be taxed, since with an IRA your taxes are deferred until years later when you start to withdraw the income. So it holds a lot of high-yield credit funds, as well as higher-yielding equity funds and business development companies (“BDCs”).

The other is a Taxable Model that uses assets that are friendlier, from a tax standpoint, in that they mostly include closed-end funds that hold corporate equities of various types, including higher-yielding utilities, infrastructure, MLPs, covered calls, and general equity. Most of the dividends from these funds are classified as “qualified” income or as long-term capital gains, which are taxed at lower rates; or, even better in many instances, are classified as “return of capital” (a highly misunderstood term) in which taxes are deferred until the asset is sold.

The “do-it-yourselfers” among our members tend to use the model portfolios as lists of suggested “candidates” on which to do their own due diligence and from which to make selections to include in their own portfolios. Our members who are more inclined to a “set-it-and-forget-it” strategy will often replicate all or most of the model and rely on our monthly reviews for any updates or changes.

Getting Real, With “Live” Portfolios

But in addition to the formal model portfolios, in August 2024 I decided to “get real” and set up a couple of smaller, simplified versions of both the Core and the Taxable models as actual separate, standalone “live” accounts at Fidelity. I review both models, sharing the exact data that Fidelity reports to me on each account, with our Inside the Income Factory members each month.

Each account is on automatic DRIP (“Dividend Reinvestment”), so the current value of each fund at any time reflects the entire total return of the account up to that moment; i.e., the sum total of the original investment plus cash distributions, adjusted for market price changes. The “Live” Core model consists of 14 funds and BDCs, comprising general equities, covered call funds, utilities and MLPs, senior loans, high-yield bonds, and CLOs.

The Taxable model consists of 10 closed-end funds, comprising traditional equity, covered call, global equity, and utility/infrastructure. While 10, or 14, funds is not a lot of holdings, each one is itself highly diversified, so there's a great deal of overall diversification in each portfolio.

The results, summarized below, for the past 17 months have been very encouraging. The total return for the Live Core model has been 12% since August 2024, which is an annualized rate of 8.5%. That's a little below our long-term target range of 9-10%, but frankly, it is quite satisfactory when we consider how credit funds have been under so much retail market pressure over the past six months (with media, bankers, and others talking about “cockroaches” invading the corporate credit markets, etc.). The funds we hold are all solid funds managed by experienced outfits, and I believe them to be reliable “through the cycle” holdings. Helping us to remain calm and confident is the solid 10.8% cash distribution that we're collecting in monthly increments and using to reinvest and compound our income stream.

Our Live Taxable model has outperformed its sibling Core Model, in terms of total return, earning an impressive 24.8% for the 17 months since inception, an annualized rate of 17.5%. Because it's limited to more tax-efficient but slightly lower yielding equity funds than the Core Model funds, we don't expect the Live Taxable model to have as high a distribution yield. However, as equities, there's the potential for greater capital gains, as we have seen recently. And the 8% yield in the taxable account is high enough to continue growing our cash income at a solid pace and to provide some sense of security during short periods when the market may be soft or down.

2026 - No Time To Be A Hero

I think 2026 will be a time to keep our heads down and not try to be a hero. There's so much uncertainty—economic, political, and global—that a strategy that emphasizes “muddling through and staying alive” seems more appropriate than one that requires issuers to thrive, grow their earnings, and boost their stock prices.

Fortunately, that's the strategy we have been pursuing for several years with our investment service members. Our model portfolios, both our “Live” ones as described here and our larger model portfolios, focus on credit investing, where all an issuer has to do is stay alive and pay its debts, and conservative equity plays, like utilities and infrastructure funds, covered call funds, and closed end equity funds that we can buy at discounts to give us an additional margin of safety.

In 2026 I'm more committed to following this strategy than ever, in part because of how I see 2026 developing so far and continuing throughout the year (highly unpredictable!), but also in part because it's the fundamental basis of our strategy to begin with. In short: Our Income Factory strategy is pretty basic and conservative (kind of like watching the grass grow, if you have the patience and have planted the right grass). But in a year like 2026, with all the uncertainties we face, I feel more comfortable with it than ever.

Obviously there's the risk of being wrong. That we're too conservative and miss a booming stock market. If I can collect my 10%-plus yield and grow my cash earnings, I can live with the risk of losing out on the bigger gains that come with taking those much bigger risks.

Conclusion

We all know how the investment media focuses almost exclusively on market price movements, because that's more transparent and widely publicized than the cash earnings that financial assets are generating. Tracking the cash income (which can actually be pretty exciting to real investors like us receiving 8-10% or more from our own investments) would be pretty uneventful to viewers of CNBC or Bloomberg TV. So there's no wonder the cable financial outlets cover the minute-to-minute changes in price indices like the Dow Jones Industrials or the S&P 500, whipping up viewer interest and excitement in it, the same way ESPN covers sports.

But we don't have to fall for it, especially if we follow the advice of legendary economist John Burr Williams and, many decades later, Woodward & Bernstein of Watergate fame, as well as others, to “follow the money.” That's essentially what our Income Factory is all about.

data.title: Globus Medical 2026 Outlook: Why M&A Math Mires The Valuation
data.description:
Overview

This is my first look at Globus Medical (GMED). This is a healthcare equipment company that was founded in 2003. It specializes in musculoskeletal products, such as fracture plating.

Notably, the company’s growth has become increasingly dependent on M&A activity. Back in early September 2023, the company merged with NuVasive. In April 2025, Globus officially acquired Nevro Corp. for approximately $250 million. In fact, when you glance at Globus’ revenues, you can see clearly the inflections of both deals (the quarters ended September 2023 and June 2025).

Note that Globus’ revenues prior to its Nervo acquisition stalled (essentially five consecutive quarters of no growth).

My purpose in the following article is to break down Globus’ valuation in light of recent trends to see if there is any hidden upside. I conclude that Globus appears reasonably valued, meriting a neutral stance given ongoing dependence on M&A to support growth.

Earnings

Earlier this week, Globus unveiled preliminary FY25 earnings, as well as FY26 guidance.

For Q4 and FY25, Globus expects approximately $823.2 million in sales (up 24.2% Y/Y) and $2.936 billion (up 16.5% Y/Y), respectively. However, Globus’ base business (which now includes NuVasive) grew 10% Y/Y in Q4. If you take a step back, its base business grew merely 4.9% Y/Y in FY25.

Nevro’s contributions have been pretty flat (Q2 $94.6M, Q3 $99.3M, and Q4 $99.9M).

Looking ahead to FY26, Globus guided revenues between $3.18 billion and $3.22 billion. This is a tight range and consistent with a very predictable business. This is not a bad thing, but just an observation. Of course, I typically cover volatile pharma companies, whereas Globus is a long-established medical technology company.

Anyways, what do its FY26 estimates imply?

For one, growth is expected to decelerate sharply versus FY25. FY26’s midpoint estimate assumes 9% Y/Y growth. Compare this with the 16.5% Y/Y growth for FY25.

This means that organic growth is likely mid-single-digits (this is inferred), assuming Nevro is roughly flat per quarter.

On the other hand, its FY26 EPS guidance of $4.30 to $4.40 implies margin expansion. This is likely a feature of synergy capture, operating leverage, and continued share repurchases.

Valuation

My revenue and FCF margin assumptions for Globus are 8% and 20%, respectively. I came to these figures after assessing recent trends.

Bear in mind that while recent quarters have hit ~27%, the company's historical FCF margin average of 16.5% has been impacted by acquisitions.

So, assuming a “steady state” of 20% accounts for future M&A integration costs without punishing them too much for their past.

GMED was actually very easy to model, which, again, speaks to the predictability of its business.

“Year 1 Revenue” represents management’s FY26 estimate.

Most everything else was based on GMED’s latest financial figures or is standard DCF methodology (e.g., Terminal Growth, Tax Rate).

As you can see, GMED appears reasonably valued per my model (undervalued by only ~4%).

When I run a Sensitivity Analysis on my DCF model, it reveals that GMED’s valuation is about equally sensitive to growth and margin.

GMED's current price implies a 7.4% growth rate (assuming all else stays the same), which appears reasonable given its organic growth rate.

Personally, I’m not seeing a lot of paths towards outperformance. In light of slowing growth, Globus is predominantly tasked with improving profitability to support its valuation.

Should growth fall to 5%, which is entirely plausible over nine years, a 23% FCF margin could support GMED's valuation. On the other hand, should GMED encounter ongoing margin pressures (perhaps it continues to resort to M&A to spur growth), a CAGR of 11% could buoy its valuation.

The reality is that, beyond mid-20s FCF margins, I don’t believe Globus has much more room to expand. We are already seeing “high conversion” economics from this mature medtech. That said, I modeled for additional expansion (relative to its last eight quarters of performance) given that Globus is boosting Nevro's profitability while stabilizing revenue.

Moreover, management’s goal is to lift adjusted gross profit back toward the mid-70s over time, which seems feasible.

My point is that Globus is unlikely to see FCF margins materially above the mid-20s. This is just an inherent feature of medtech (high fixed costs, salesforce-driven growth), and the company already outperforms its peers on profitability.

Durability

A key question moving forward for Globus is durability. Looking back to Q3, there was a significant change in operating leverage, which likely inspired the stock rally.

Q3 2025 operating income was $137.4 million on $769 million in sales versus $48.1 million on $625.7 million in sales in Q3 2024. Q3 2025 cost of sales (ex-intangibles) actually declined Y/Y even as revenue rose.

The focus moving forward is whether this is repeatable as integration proceeds versus being period-specific (e.g., mix/timing, acquisition accounting effects, restructuring cadence).

That said, management’s FY26 guidance does support the “durability thesis” because it implies EPS growth between ~11.7% and ~17.34% (which outpaces revenue growth projections).

Final Words

Globus’ headline growth is still being flattered by acquisitions, while the base business has been mid-single-digit. While the company is generating strong cash flow, the margin runway is limited. So far, M&A has worked wonders, but its dependence typically doesn't make for a great investment over the long haul (GMED's stock is ~+40% in the past five years, materially underperforming the index).

From here, Globus’ path forward appears to offer only incremental value creation via full integration, keeping U.S. spine growing above market, stabilizing Nevro/extracting efficiencies, and share buybacks.

I think the market will likely need either a re-acceleration in organic growth or a clear step-up in margin to justify additional multiple expansion (beyond November's re-rating). In my view, neither appears imminent.

Net-net, GMED appears reasonably valued, but with a narrow path to outperformance, meriting a cautious Hold rating.

data.title: Ondas: Let Mushrooming Valuation Cool
data.description:
Ondas Holdings Inc. (ONDS) spent the last couple of quarters building up a company with a viable autonomous drone defense system. Not only has the stock soared, but also the company has raised a substantial amount of capital via equity raises to invest in the bright future. My investment thesis is more Neutral on the stock as the valuation has mushroomed somewhat disconnected from the actual financial targets.

Limited Bookings

Ondas ended Q3 with a backlog of only $22 million. The company guided to an initial 2026 revenue target of $110 million, while the target is an increase of around 200% from the 2025 target, the number is still relatively small.

The counter-UAS sector has the potential for massive increase in future orders. The war in Ukraine has escalated the need for a counter-drone defense solution, and Ondas offers a leading solution in this growing sector.

On the Q3'25 earnings call, Sentrycs CEO Tal Cohen outlined the market potential as follows:

When we look at the broader counter-UAS market, the opportunity ahead of us is extremely significant. Global demand for counter drone technologies is projected to grow from roughly $2.4 billion in 2024 to over $10.5 billion by 2030, representing a 27% compound annual growth rate. This growth is driven by the rapid escalation of drone threats across defense, homeland security and critical infrastructure sectors and by increasing government funding and regulation worldwide as we are seeing strong momentum across all regions.

In reality, one can envision counter drone systems growing to a far larger market than just $10 billion. The whole Western world will need systems to protect borders and sky perimeters from drones.

For now, though, Ondas only reported Q3 revenues of $10 million and is targeting Q4 revenues of $15 million. While the company set an initial 2026 revenue target of $110 million, the consensus analyst estimates are already up at $140 million.

Ondas has potentially won a prime contract with Israel for a $1.7 billion smart border project along Jordan. Israel is leaning heavily into autonomous technologies for border security and apparently has awarded the contract to Airobotics, a company acquired by Ondas back in 2023. So far, Ondas hasn't announced any financial details.

Trump Bump

Ondas got an additional big boost this last week, with President Trump pushing the narrative of the US boosting defense spending to $1.5 trillion. The government would have to approve a 50% boost to spending on military equipment, while at the same time Trump has complained legacy defense contractors aren’t increasing production fast enough.

The stock soared to $14 after ending last year below $10. Ondas is back to record price levels, while the market cap is at vastly higher levels. The market hasn’t fully grasped the increased valuation from Ondas selling millions of shares and raising $855 million in new capital in 2025 via 4 equity offerings and warrant/option exercises.

Ondas had seen the market cap surge to $5.2 billion before the company completed another capital raise of $1 billion before the market opened. The stock was only worth $3 billion around October when Ondas hit the previous high and prior to the last big capital raise of $425 million.

As well, Ondas had already pre-funded what appeared like the next capital raise via 73.9 million warrants. At an exercise price of $20, the company will raise another $1.5 billion on just under a 50% gain in the stock.

This additional $1 billion capital raise immediately pushed the market cap to $6.2 billion. Ondas has again pre-funded another capital raise with the issuance of 121.6 million warrants at an exercise price of $28 for the potential capital raise of $3.5 billion.

At $20, Ondas quickly soars to a market value of over $10 billion and the market cap hits around $17.5 billion at $28, though the company would raise an incredible additional $5 billion in the process. The 2027 revenue target is currently only $250 million, but the surging demand for drones could lead to significant upside to targets, yet the stock valuation already appears to bake in large growth.

The company only reported an EBITDA loss of $8.8 million in Q3. At this point, Ondas doesn't appear to face any cash burn from operation issues, with revenues ramping up substantially going forward.

The biggest risk is Ondas not having any established long-term leadership in the drone market. The company isn't guaranteed to maintain any technology lead, while the market valuation is already assuming several years of massive growth.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Ondas has a very promising growth story. The autonomous systems provider has already seen the stock surge to the point Ondas hits a wall up around $20 with the additional warrants and the wide gap potential in this valuation and the 2026 initial sales target of just $110 million.
